content.text: Now update `app-deploy.yml` to actually build and deploy the frontend (review blocker #2) and add `workflow_dispatch` for ordered triggering:

input.content:
name: app-deploy
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["app/**", ".github/workflows/app-deploy.yml"]
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"
          cache: "npm"
          cache-dependency-path: app/package-lock.json

      - name: Install + CI gate (lint / typecheck / test)
        working-directory: app
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm run lint
          npm run typecheck
          npm test

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Deploy Amplify Gen 2 backend (regenerates amplify_outputs.json)
        working-directory: app
        run: npx ampx pipeline-deploy --branch main --app-id ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }}

      - name: Publish Amplify hosting URL to SSM (read from the API, never string-templated)
        run: |
          DOMAIN=$(aws amplify get-app --app-id ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }} --query 'app.defaultDomain' --output text)
          URL="https://main.${DOMAIN}"
          case "$URL" in https://main.*.amplifyapp.com) ;; *) echo "Bad Amplify URL: $URL" && exit 1 ;; esac
          aws ssm put-parameter --name /reviewlensai/amplify/url --type String --overwrite --value "$URL"

      - name: Build frontend (bundles real outputs + validator URL if the scraper is deployed)
        working-directory: app
        run: |
          VURL=$(aws ssm get-parameter --name /reviewlensai/scraper/validatorUrl --query Parameter.Value --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "")
          echo "VITE_VALIDATOR_URL=${VURL:-<not set yet>}"
          VITE_VALIDATOR_URL="$VURL" npm run build

      - name: Deploy frontend to Amplify Hosting (manual zip deployment)
        working-directory: app
        env:
          APP_ID: ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }}
        run: |
          (cd dist && zip -qr ../dist.zip .)
          OUT=$(aws amplify create-deployment --app-id "$APP_ID" --branch-name main)
          JOB_ID=$(echo "$OUT" | jq -r '.jobId')
          UPLOAD_URL=$(echo "$OUT" | jq -r '.zipUploadUrl')
          curl -s -H "Content-Type: application/zip" --upload-file dist.zip "$UPLOAD_URL"
          aws amplify start-deployment --app-id "$APP_ID" --branch-name main --job-id "$JOB_ID"
          echo "Frontend deployment started (job $JOB_ID)."

name: app-deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["app/**", ".github/workflows/app-deploy.yml"]
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: app
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"
          cache: "npm"
          cache-dependency-path: app/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm test
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - name: Deploy Amplify backend (pipeline)
        run: npx ampx pipeline-deploy --branch main --app-id ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }}
      - name: Publish Amplify hosting URL to SSM (read from the API, never string-templated)
        run: |
          DOMAIN=$(aws amplify get-app --app-id ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }} --query 'app.defaultDomain' --output text)
          URL="https://main.${DOMAIN}"
          # Fail loudly if the URL is malformed — it becomes the Validator CORS origin (spec §6).
          case "$URL" in https://main.*.amplifyapp.com) ;; *) echo "Bad Amplify URL: $URL" && exit 1 ;; esac
          aws ssm put-parameter --name /reviewlensai/amplify/url --type String --overwrite --value "$URL"

content.text: Now update `scraper-deploy.yml`: add `workflow_dispatch`, and replace the broken `amplify start-job RELEASE` step (which doesn't apply to manual-zip deploys) with a trigger that re-runs `app-deploy` so the frontend rebuilds with the now-published `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`.

input.content:
name: scraper-deploy
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["scraper/**", ".github/workflows/scraper-deploy.yml"]
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
  actions: write
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Unit tests + lint
        working-directory: scraper
        run: |
          python -m pip install -e ".[dev]"
          python -m pytest -q
          ruff check src tests scripts

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: "${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}"
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Build Lambda asset (CLEAN build dir — never the source tree)
        working-directory: scraper
        run: |
          rm -rf build && mkdir -p build
          cp -r src/reviewlensai_scraper build/
          pip install requests -t build/
        # boto3 is in the Lambda runtime; only `requests` is vendored into the clean build/
        # dir. This matches Code.fromAsset("../build") in the CDK stack.

      - name: CDK unit tests + deploy (reads app SSM params at synth — app must be deployed first)
        working-directory: scraper/cdk
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm test           # jest stack assertions (../build exists from the prior step)
          npx cdk deploy --require-approval never
        # The CDK stack writes /reviewlensai/scraper/validatorUrl to SSM.

      - name: Trigger app frontend rebuild (picks up the new validatorUrl)
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run app-deploy.yml --ref main \
            || echo "Could not auto-trigger app-deploy; re-run it manually to bake VITE_VALIDATOR_URL."

name: scraper-deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["scraper/**", ".github/workflows/scraper-deploy.yml"]
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Unit tests + lint
        working-directory: scraper
        run: |
          python -m pip install -e ".[dev]"
          python -m pytest -q
          ruff check src tests

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: "${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}"
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Build Lambda asset (CLEAN build dir — never the source tree)
        working-directory: scraper
        run: |
          rm -rf build && mkdir -p build
          cp -r src/reviewlensai_scraper build/
          pip install requests -t build/
        # boto3 is in the Lambda runtime; only `requests` is vendored into the clean build/
        # dir. This matches Code.fromAsset("../build") in the CDK stack. The source tree is
        # never mutated so the asset hash stays stable across runs.

      - name: CDK unit tests + deploy (reads app SSM params at synth — app must be deployed first)
        working-directory: scraper/cdk
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm test           # jest stack assertions (../build exists from the prior step)
          npx cdk deploy --require-approval never

      - name: Feed Validator URL back to the app (spec §6 step 3)
        run: |
          VURL=$(aws ssm get-parameter \
            --name /reviewlensai/scraper/validatorUrl \
            --query Parameter.Value \
            --output text)
          aws amplify update-branch \
            --app-id "${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }}" \
            --branch-name main \
            --environment-variables "VITE_VALIDATOR_URL=${VURL}"
          aws amplify start-job \
            --app-id "${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }}" \
            --branch-name main \
            --job-type RELEASE

content.text: The GitHub OIDC provider already exists. Let me create the repo-scoped deploy role and attach permissions (PoC-broad — I'll note it should be tightened for production).

input.command:
export MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1
cat > /tmp/trust.json <<'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Principal": { "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::REDACTED_ACCT:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com" },
    "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
    "Condition": {
      "StringEquals": { "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud": "sts.amazonaws.com" },
      "StringLike": { "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub": "repo:NicholasACTran/reviewlensai:*" }
    }
  }]
}
EOF
echo "=== create role ==="
ROLE_ARN=$(aws iam create-role --role-name reviewlensai-gha-deploy \
  --assume-role-policy-document file:///tmp/trust.json \
  --description "GitHub Actions OIDC deploy role for reviewlensai (PoC: broad perms)" \
  --query 'Role.Arn' --output text 2>&1)
echo "role ARN: $ROLE_ARN"
echo "=== attach AdministratorAccess (PoC) ==="
aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name reviewlensai-gha-deploy \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess 2>&1 && echo "attached"
echo "$ROLE_ARN" > /tmp/reviewlensai_role_arn.txt

input.command:
cd "C:/Users/nicho/OneDrive/Documents/Projects/reviewlensai" && gh pr create --base main --head feature/phase-1 --title "Phase 1: Scraper + basic App" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
Implements Phase 1 (contract-first): the Steam-review **scraper** (two async Python Lambdas + S3 + CDK) and the **app** (Amplify Gen 2 React SPA) coupled by the shared \`Job\` row.

## What's here
- **App** (\`app/\`): Vite+React+TS SPA, Amplify Gen 2 \`Job\` schema (AppSync+DynamoDB), real-time \`observeQuery\`, URL box → animated waiting → nominal screen, MSW + FakeAmplifyClient local-sim. 19 tests.
- **Scraper** (\`scraper/\`): Validator Lambda (Function URL, validates + async-invokes), Scraper Lambda (paginates ≤10k reviews → S3, conditional AppSync transitions, \`ScrapeSucceeded\` event), CDK stack (DLQ, reserved concurrency, custom bus, alarms). 21 pytest + 5 CDK tests.
- **Process**: spec + plans under \`docs/\`, reviewed by the Devil's-Advocate panel (spec rounds 1–2, plan rounds 1–2, final whole-PR review) per CLAUDE.md; all blockers resolved.
- **CI/CD**: GitHub Actions deploy workflows per domain (app: Amplify Gen 2 backend + frontend hosting; scraper: CDK), wired via SSM cross-domain contract.

## Verification
45 automated checks green locally (app 19, scraper 21, CDK 5) — lint/typecheck/build clean. Backend deploy validated on real AWS via an ampx sandbox.

Deferred to Phase 2/3: analytics, chat, the \`fetch_review_summary\` micro-optimization (noted in code).
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -3

3	from dataclasses import dataclass, field

5	import requests
6	
7	UA = "ReviewLensAI/1.0 (+https://github.com/NicholasACTran/reviewlensai)"
8	BASE = "https://store.steampowered.com"
9	TIMEOUT = 15
10	
11	class SteamError(Exception): ...
12	class SteamReviewsUnavailable(SteamError): ...  # success:false mid-scrape (spec §4.2)
13	
14	@dataclass
15	class ScrapeResult:
16	    total_reviews: int
17	    total_positive: int
18	    reviews: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
19	    @property
20	    def scraped(self) -> int: return len(self.reviews)
21	    @property
22	    def pct_positive(self) -> float | None:
23	        return None if self.total_reviews == 0 else self.total_positive / self.total_reviews
24	
25	def fetch_appdetails(app_id: str, cc: str = "us", lang: str = "english") -> tuple[bool, dict | None]:
26	    r = requests.get(f"{BASE}/api/appdetails", params={"appids": app_id, "cc": cc, "l": lang},
27	                     headers={"User-Agent": UA}, timeout=TIMEOUT)
28	    r.raise_for_status()
29	    node = r.json().get(app_id, {})
30	    if not node.get("success"):
31	        return False, None
32	    return True, node["data"]
33	
34	_KEEP = ("recommendationid", "language", "review", "timestamp_created", "timestamp_updated",
35	         "voted_up", "votes_up", "votes_funny", "steam_purchase", "received_for_free",
36	         "written_during_early_access")
37	_KEEP_AUTHOR = ("playtime_at_review", "playtime_forever")
38	
39	def trim_review(raw: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
40	    out = {k: raw[k] for k in _KEEP if k in raw}
41	    author = raw.get("author", {})
42	    out["author"] = {k: author[k] for k in _KEEP_AUTHOR if k in author}
43	    return out
44	
45	def fetch_review_summary(app_id: str) -> tuple[int, int]:
46	    """Totals come from a DEDICATED summary request. With `filter=recent` Steam does NOT
47	    populate query_summary.total_reviews/total_positive; `filter=all&num_per_page=0` does."""
48	    body = _request_reviews(app_id, {"num_per_page": "0", "filter": "all"})
49	    qs = body.get("query_summary", {})
50	    return qs.get("total_reviews", 0), qs.get("total_positive", 0)
51	
52	def scrape_reviews(app_id: str, max_reviews: int, delay_s: float = 0.75) -> ScrapeResult:
53	    total_reviews, total_positive = fetch_review_summary(app_id)   # totals (filter=all)
54	    result = ScrapeResult(total_reviews=total_reviews, total_positive=total_positive)
55	    seen: set[str] = set()
56	    cursor = "*"
57	    while True:                                                    # bodies (filter=recent)
58	        page = _request_reviews(app_id, {"num_per_page": "100", "filter": "recent", "cursor": cursor})
59	        if not page.get("success", 1):
60	            raise SteamReviewsUnavailable(f"appreviews success=false for {app_id}")
61	        for raw in page.get("reviews", []):
62	            rid = str(raw.get("recommendationid"))
63	            if rid in seen:
64	                continue
65	            seen.add(rid)
66	            result.reviews.append(trim_review(raw))
67	            if len(result.reviews) >= max_reviews:
68	                return result
69	        next_cursor = page.get("cursor")
70	        if not page.get("reviews") or next_cursor is None or next_cursor == cursor:
71	            return result            # empty page OR repeated cursor = end of stream
72	        cursor = next_cursor
73	        if delay_s:
74	            time.sleep(delay_s)
75	
76	def _request_reviews(app_id: str, extra: dict[str, str]) -> dict[str, Any]:
77	    """GET /appreviews with retry on 429/5xx. `requests` URL-encodes params once (incl. cursor)."""
78	    url = f"{BASE}/appreviews/{app_id}"
79	    params = {"json": "1", "language": "all", "purchase_type": "all", **extra}
80	    last_exc: Exception | None = None
81	    for attempt in range(3):                       # spec §4.2: max 3 tries on 429/5xx
82	        try:
83	            r = requests.get(url, params=params, headers={"User-Agent": UA}, timeout=TIMEOUT)
84	            if r.status_code in (429, 500, 502, 503, 504):
85	                raise SteamError(f"HTTP {r.status_code}")
86	            r.raise_for_status()
87	            return r.json()
88	        except (requests.RequestException, SteamError) as e:
89	            last_exc = e
90	            time.sleep(0.5 * (attempt + 1))
91	    raise SteamError(f"appreviews failed after retries: {last_exc}")

aiTitle: Set up Steam reviews analytics app repository

content.text: The scraper CDK deploy got all the way to CloudFormation (tests passed, synth read the SSM params fine) but failed on one thing: **`OPTIONS` is not a valid Function-URL CORS `allowedMethod`** — the valid set is `[GET, PUT, HEAD, POST, PATCH, DELETE, *]`. Ironically, the final review told me to *add* `OPTIONS`, but Lambda Function URLs auto-handle the OPTIONS preflight, so listing it is invalid. CDK synth/jest don't validate this enum (only CloudFormation does), which is why local tests passed. Let me fix it.

content.content:
72	    const url = validatorFn.addFunctionUrl({
73	      authType: FunctionUrlAuthType.NONE,
74	      // OPTIONS must be allowed or the browser preflight is rejected at the Function URL layer
75	      // before the handler's OPTIONS branch runs.
76	      cors: {
77	        allowedOrigins: [amplifyUrl],
78	        allowedMethods: [lambda.HttpMethod.POST, lambda.HttpMethod.OPTIONS],
79	        allowedHeaders: ["content-type"],
80	      },
81	    });
82	
83	    // Alarms (spec §4.4): DLQ depth + scraper errors. NOT_BREACHING avoids INSUFFICIENT_DATA noise
The file C:\Users\nicho\OneDrive\Documents\Projects\reviewlensai\scraper\cdk\lib\scraper-stack.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: -      // OPTIONS must be allowed or the browser preflight is rejected at the Function URL layer
-      // before the handler's OPTIONS branch runs.
+      // The Function URL auto-handles the CORS OPTIONS preflight from this config — OPTIONS is NOT
+      // a valid allowedMethods value (CloudFormation rejects it); listing POST + the allowed
+      // origin/headers is what makes the browser preflight succeed.

content.text: The validator Lambda actually **worked server-side** — it created a Job row (`edac80e7…`). So the browser got a response it couldn't *read* → a **CORS response failure**. The likely cause: two CORS layers both setting `access-control-allow-origin` (the Function URL CORS config *and* the handler's manual headers) → duplicate header → browser blocks. Let me confirm by inspecting the response headers (using an invalid URL so I don't kick off a scrape):

input.command:
cd "C:/Users/nicho/OneDrive/Documents/Projects/reviewlensai"
VURL="https://ujperyyrlse6fcibiexqsxwbmu0jcend.lambda-url.us-east-1.on.aws/"
echo "=== response headers (invalid URL → 400 path, same CORS code) ==="
curl -s -i -X POST -H "Origin: https://main.d1zccfa4rl4qf6.amplifyapp.com" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"url":"notasteamurl"}' "$VURL" 2>&1 | grep -iE "^HTTP|access-control-allow-origin|access-control-allow-methods"
input.description: Inspect validator CORS response headers for duplicates

content.text: **Confirmed: duplicate `access-control-allow-origin` headers** — both the Function URL CORS config *and* the handler's `_resp` set it, and browsers reject multiple ACAO values. The fix: let the Function URL CORS own CORS; strip the handler's manual CORS headers. Let me read and fix `_resp`.

content.content:
28	        "access-control-allow-headers": "content-type",
29	    }, "body": json.dumps(body)}
30	
31	def _parse_app_id(url: str) -> str | None:
32	    try:
33	        u = urlparse(url)
34	    except ValueError:
35	        return None
36	    if u.scheme not in ("http", "https") or u.netloc != "store.steampowered.com":
37	        return None
38	    m = _APP_RE.match(u.path)
39	    return m.group(1) if m else None
40	
41	def handler(event: dict, _context) -> dict:

21	    return boto3.client("lambda")
22	
23	def _resp(status: int, body: dict) -> dict:
24	    return {"statusCode": status, "headers": {
25	        "content-type": "application/json",
26	        "access-control-allow-origin": ALLOWED_ORIGIN,
27	        "access-control-allow-methods": "POST,OPTIONS",
28	        "access-control-allow-headers": "content-type",
29	    }, "body": json.dumps(body)}

content.text:
Live observation working — the current job is `RUNNING` (name "Stardew Valley" already seeded), and notably the **first attempt's job (`edac80e7`) actually completed on the backend** (`worker_complete`, scraped=10000, S3 written) — confirming the earlier failure was purely the FE CORS-read issue, not the backend. That's exactly the FE-vs-backend separability the observability practice is for.

Let me wait for the current scrape (~2 min for the 10k cap) to finish and watch the FE flip via `observeQuery`:

input.command:
cd "C:/Users/nicho/OneDrive/Documents/Projects/reviewlensai"
export MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1
JOB="a9c70456-eb8f-4693-9a1f-63b07a3296ab"
TABLE="Job-xfj4tgpqsvgfbaxoyybuekelui-NONE"
echo "polling backend until terminal..."
until S=$(aws dynamodb get-item --table-name "$TABLE" --region us-east-1 --key "{\"id\":{\"S\":\"$JOB\"}}" --query 'Item.status.S' --output text 2>/dev/null); [ "$S" = "SUCCEEDED" ] || [ "$S" = "FAILED" ]; do echo "  status=$S"; sleep 20; done
echo "=== FINAL Job row ==="
aws dynamodb get-item --table-name "$TABLE" --region us-east-1 --key "{\"id\":{\"S\":\"$JOB\"}}" \
  --query 'Item.{status:status.S,gameName:gameName.S,total:totalReviews.N,pct:pctPositive.N,scraped:scrapedReviews.N,s3Key:s3Key.S}' --output json 2>&1

content.text: Working tree clean, all on `main`, deployed and E2E-validated. Let me save the hard-won deployment gotchas to memory for next time, then summarize.

input.content:
---
name: reference_deploy_gotchas
description: Deployment gotchas hit while shipping Phase 1 (Amplify Gen 2 + CDK via GitHub Actions)
metadata:
  type: reference
---

Hard-won fixes from the Phase 1 staging deploy (all resolved):

- **Lambda Function URL CORS owns CORS — do NOT also set CORS headers in the handler.** Doing both yields **duplicate `access-control-allow-origin` headers**, which browsers reject (request succeeds server-side, but the FE `fetch` throws → generic error). The handler should return only `content-type`; the Function URL `cors:{allowedOrigins,allowedMethods,allowedHeaders}` handles everything incl. the OPTIONS preflight.
- **`OPTIONS` is NOT a valid Function URL `allowedMethods` value** (valid: GET/PUT/HEAD/POST/PATCH/DELETE/*). CloudFormation rejects it; `cdk synth`/jest do NOT catch it (only the CFN changeset does). The preflight is auto-handled — just list POST.
- **git-bash mangles `/reviewlensai/...` SSM names** (MSYS path conversion → Windows path → "must be fully qualified"). Prefix SSM CLI calls with `export MSYS_NO_PATHCONV=1`. (boto3 calls are unaffected.)
- **`npm ci` needs an in-sync lockfile.** Commit `package-lock.json` and re-run `npm install` after any dep change or CI fails with "package.json and package-lock.json not in sync."
- **Amplify Gen 2 frontend config:** use a STATIC `import outputs from "../amplify_outputs.json"` (Vite bundles it; the deploy regenerates it before `vite build`). A runtime dynamic-path import does NOT load on the deployed SPA. Commit a sanitized placeholder so local/CI builds + typecheck pass (real file stays gitignored).
- **PUSH before merging a PR.** Local commits not pushed won't be in the merged `main` (cost a re-fix cycle).
- **Deploy order (spec §6):** app (AppSync+SSM) → scraper (CDK reads SSM at synth, writes validatorUrl) → app rebuild (bakes `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`). Workflows are `main`-push + `workflow_dispatch`; on first merge both fire, so cancel scraper and run it after app.
- Deploy infra: OIDC role `reviewlensai-gha-deploy` (AdministratorAccess, PoC-broad — tighten for prod), secrets `AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN`/`AMPLIFY_APP_ID` (app `d1zccfa4rl4qf6`). See [[reference_aws_deploy_access]].

content.text:
Phase 1 is **built, reviewed, deployed via GitHub Actions, and end-to-end validated against real AWS.** Here's the complete picture.

## ✅ Live deployment
- **App:** https://main.d1zccfa4rl4qf6.amplifyapp.com (Amplify Hosting; AppSync + DynamoDB `Job` table)
- **Scraper:** Validator Function URL + async Scraper Lambda + S3 + EventBridge bus + DLQ/alarms (CDK)
- **Deployed entirely through the `.github/workflows/*-deploy.yml` GitHub Actions** (per CLAUDE.md/OVERVIEW), using an OIDC deploy role — no ad-hoc CLI deploys.

## ✅ Playwright PM E2E (screenshots in `screenshots/`)
Driven in a real browser, paired with live backend observation (Job-row + Scraper-log watching):
| Flow | Result |
|---|---|
| Paste Steam URL → scrape → nominal | **Stardew Valley · 1,009,671 reviews · 98% positive · $14.99** rendered via real-time `observeQuery` |
| Backend scrape | 10,000 reviews (cap) → S3, `Job` PENDING→RUNNING→SUCCEEDED |
| Invalid URL | inline "That's not a Steam game URL." |
| Non-existent game | inline "We couldn't find that game on Steam." |

The observability practice paid off immediately: when the first submit showed a FE error, backend watching proved the Lambda had *succeeded* — isolating it to a CORS-response bug, not a backend failure.

## 🔧 Deploy-phase bugs found & fixed (only surfaced against real AWS)
1. **Duplicate `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`** (Function URL CORS + handler both set it → browser blocked) — handler CORS removed.
2. **`OPTIONS` invalid as a Function-URL CORS method** (CFN-only validation; synth/jest passed) — removed.
3. **`main.tsx` runtime config import** wouldn't load in prod — switched to static bundled import + placeholder.
4. **Lockfile out of sync** (`npm ci` failure) and **3 unpushed commits** that the initial PR merge missed — both reconciled.

## 📋 Full session arc
Brainstorm → spec (DA panel ×2, blocker-free) → 2 plans (DA panel ×2 + final whole-PR review) → **18 TDD tasks via subagents** (45 unit/integration checks green) → GitHub Actions deploy → live E2E. Everything on `main`.

## One remaining workflow step
CLAUDE.md step 9 says to **save the session transcript to `ai-transcripts/`** (scrubbing keys/secrets) now that the work is validated. Want me to do that? Note I can't access this conversation's raw transcript directly — if you can export it (or point me at it), I'll scrub and file it; otherwise I can write a structured session summary doc in its place.

A couple of small follow-ups I noted but deliberately didn't block on: the `fetch_review_summary` micro-optimization (one redundant Steam call — flagged in code), and tightening the PoC-broad deploy role for any future production use.

# Scraper - Architecture

## AWS resources (CDK: `scraper/cdk/lib/scraper-stack.ts`)

- **Validator Lambda** with public Function URL (`AuthType: NONE`, CORS allow-list from SSM `/reviewlensai/amplify/url`). Validates URL shape + does a HEAD pre-check against steam.com. 10s timeout.
- **AWS Batch (Fargate)** compute environment (1 vCPU, 2 GB) + job queue + **one job definition**: `WorkerJobDefinition` (GAME, 5min).
- **ECR repository** `reviewlensai-worker` — single image, used by the job definition.
- **S3 bucket** for raw scrape output, `jobs/{jobId}/{app_id}.json`. 30-day lifecycle on `jobs/*`.
- **JobWatchdog Lambda** + EventBridge rule filtered to `aws.batch` `Batch Job State Change` events with `status: FAILED` on our queue. Flips orphaned `RUNNING` `Job` rows to `FAILED` via a conditional AppSync mutation (`condition: { status: { eq: 'RUNNING' } }`).
- **CloudWatch logs**, 14-day retention, on Validator + Batch task + Watchdog.

## Runtime behaviors

- **Validator HEAD pre-check** is best-effort. Only `HTTP 404/410` and DNS/connect failures fail fast as `Game does not exist.`. Anti-bot blocks (403/503), timeouts, and unrecognized errors pass through. Env var `ENABLE_HEAD_PRECHECK=false` disables it without redeploy.
- **AppSync is the canonical write API for every `Job` row.** Validator, Worker, and Watchdog all write via mutations with API key auth. No direct DynamoDB writes.
- **Networking.** Default VPC, public subnets, `assignPublicIp: true` on Fargate. No NAT.
- **Observability.** Structured JSON logs with `jobId` per line.

# Scraper — Context

## Purpose

Scrape Steam user review data for a given game URL and land the
raw data in S3 for downstream analytics and chat.

## Domain Boundary

- **Trigger:** Frontend (`app/`) initiates a scrape job via a Lambda entrypoint.
- **Compute:** AWS Batch job runs the scraping workload.
- **Output:** Raw review data written to S3 under a job-scoped prefix.
- **Downstream:** On job completion, emits an event that triggers the Analytics
  Batch job (see `analytics/docs/CONTEXT.md`).
- **Frontend integration:** A small set of Lambdas lets the app trigger jobs,
  poll job status, and query the resulting S3 data.

## Tech Stack (planned)

- AWS Batch (job runner)
- AWS S3 (storage)
- AWS Lambda (trigger / status / query)
- AWS CDK (IaC)
- Language: Python

content.content:
1	# App - Architecture
2	
3	## Domain Resources
4	
5	The App Domain contains the following main resources:
6	
7	- A React + Vite SPA deployed via AWS Amplify Hosting
8	- An AppSync GraphQL API auto-generated from the Amplify Data Schema
9	- A DynamoDB `Job` table (Amplify-managed, PAY_PER_REQUEST, TTL on `expiresAt`) that stores per-job state shared between the app and the scraper domain
10	- The Amplify Gen 2 backend definition that provisions all of the above
11	
12	The app is a pure consumer of every other domain: it submits to the scraper's
13	Validator Lambda Function URL and otherwise reads state from the `Job` row in
14	DynamoDB via AppSync.
15	
16	## Real-time updates
17	
18	The frontend subscribes to a single `Job` row by id via Amplify Data's
19	`client.models.Job.observeQuery({ id })`. AppSync handles the subscription
20	plumbing; DynamoDB Streams are not used.
21	
22	**Backend writers call AppSync mutations directly.** The Validator Lambda and
23	the Batch worker both call AppSync over HTTPS, authenticating with the
24	Amplify-issued AppSync API key (`x-api-key` header), and invoke `createJob` /
25	`updateJob` mutations. The frontend's subscription fires whenever any writer
26	mutates the row.
27	
28	## Authorization model
29	
30	The `Job` schema declares a single authorization mode:
31	
32	- `allow.publicApiKey()` — used by the browser SPA, the Validator Lambda, and
33	  the Batch worker. The API key is generated by Amplify, shipped in the
34	  Amplify-generated `amplify_outputs.json` for the SPA, and written to SSM
35	  Parameter Store (`/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey`) for the scraper domain to
36	  consume.
37	
38	This is a deliberate PoC trade-off: the API key is exposed in the FE bundle,
39	which means anyone with the key can read or mutate the `Job` table. The PoC
40	holds no PII and no sensitive data; this is acceptable. A real app would
41	introduce Cognito + per-mutation Schema-level `allow.resource(...)` or
42	`allow.authenticated('userPools')` to lock down writes to trusted
43	principals.
44	
45	The original spec called for `allow.authenticated('iam')` on backend writers
46	plus SigV4 signing. That was simplified during  execution because the
47	Amplify Gen 2 IAM auth pathway requires either a Cognito Identity Pool (which
48	this project does not define) or a CDK escape hatch to add `AWS_IAM` as an
49	additional auth provider. Neither was necessary for the PoC.
50	
51	## Cross-domain contract
52	
53	The app's Amplify backend writes four SSM parameters at deploy time, which
54	the scraper domain reads at synth time:
55	
56	- `/reviewlensai/appsync/url` — AppSync GraphQL endpoint
57	- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiId` — AppSync API ID
58	- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey` — AppSync API key (for backend writers)
59	- `/reviewlensai/amplify/url` — the deployed hosting URL (used by the
60	  scraper's Validator Lambda Function URL for the CORS allow-list, and by
61	  Playwright E2E as the base URL)
62	
63	The app receives the Validator's URL via the Amplify branch environment
64	variable `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`, set by the scraper deploy workflow after the
65	scraper stack creates the Lambda Function URL.
66	
67	## Local development
68	
69	The SPA runs against MSW (handlers for the Validator URL) and a
70	`FakeAmplifyClient` (in-memory `Job` model that simulates the
71	`PENDING → RUNNING → SUCCEEDED` lifecycle on a timer). No AWS resources are
72	touched in local development. Staging is the only integration surface.
73	
74	## Observability
75	
76	CloudWatch log group for the Amplify build/deploy with 14-day retention.

file.content:
# App - Architecture

## Domain Resources

The App Domain contains the following main resources:

- A React + Vite SPA deployed via AWS Amplify Hosting
- An AppSync GraphQL API auto-generated from the Amplify Data Schema
- A DynamoDB `Job` table (Amplify-managed, PAY_PER_REQUEST, TTL on `expiresAt`) that stores per-job state shared between the app and the scraper domain
- The Amplify Gen 2 backend definition that provisions all of the above

The app is a pure consumer of every other domain: it submits to the scraper's
Validator Lambda Function URL and otherwise reads state from the `Job` row in
DynamoDB via AppSync.

## Real-time updates

The frontend subscribes to a single `Job` row by id via Amplify Data's
`client.models.Job.observeQuery({ id })`. AppSync handles the subscription
plumbing; DynamoDB Streams are not used.

**Backend writers call AppSync mutations directly.** The Validator Lambda and
the Batch worker both call AppSync over HTTPS, authenticating with the
Amplify-issued AppSync API key (`x-api-key` header), and invoke `createJob` /
`updateJob` mutations. The frontend's subscription fires whenever any writer
mutates the row.

## Authorization model

The `Job` schema declares a single authorization mode:

- `allow.publicApiKey()` — used by the browser SPA, the Validator Lambda, and
  the Batch worker. The API key is generated by Amplify, shipped in the
  Amplify-generated `amplify_outputs.json` for the SPA, and written to SSM
  Parameter Store (`/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey`) for the scraper domain to
  consume.

This is a deliberate PoC trade-off: the API key is exposed in the FE bundle,
which means anyone with the key can read or mutate the `Job` table. The PoC
holds no PII and no sensitive data; this is acceptable. A real app would
introduce Cognito + per-mutation Schema-level `allow.resource(...)` or
`allow.authenticated('userPools')` to lock down writes to trusted
principals.

The original spec called for `allow.authenticated('iam')` on backend writers
plus SigV4 signing. That was simplified during  execution because the
Amplify Gen 2 IAM auth pathway requires either a Cognito Identity Pool (which
this project does not define) or a CDK escape hatch to add `AWS_IAM` as an
additional auth provider. Neither was necessary for the PoC.

## Cross-domain contract

The app's Amplify backend writes four SSM parameters at deploy time, which
the scraper domain reads at synth time:

- `/reviewlensai/appsync/url` — AppSync GraphQL endpoint
- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiId` — AppSync API ID
- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey` — AppSync API key (for backend writers)
- `/reviewlensai/amplify/url` — the deployed hosting URL (used by the
  scraper's Validator Lambda Function URL for the CORS allow-list, and by
  Playwright E2E as the base URL)

The app receives the Validator's URL via the Amplify branch environment
variable `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`, set by the scraper deploy workflow after the
scraper stack creates the Lambda Function URL.

## Local development

The SPA runs against MSW (handlers for the Validator URL) and a
`FakeAmplifyClient` (in-memory `Job` model that simulates the
`PENDING → RUNNING → SUCCEEDED` lifecycle on a timer). No AWS resources are
touched in local development. Staging is the only integration surface.

## Observability

CloudWatch log group for the Amplify build/deploy with 14-day retention.

content.text: Now I'll rewrite the scraper docs to as-built (Lambdas, not Batch) and correct the app/overview docs. Starting with `scraper/docs/CONTEXT.md`:

input.content:
# Scraper — Context

## Purpose

Scrape Steam user review data for a given game URL and land the
raw data in S3 for downstream analytics and chat.

## Domain Boundary

- **Trigger:** Frontend (`app/`) POSTs a Steam URL to the **Validator Lambda**
  (public Function URL).
- **Compute:** Two Python Lambdas — a fast synchronous **Validator** and an
  asynchronous **Scraper** (no AWS Batch; replaced by Lambda for simplicity —
  see `ARCHITECTURE.md`).
- **Output:** Raw review JSON written to S3 under a job-scoped prefix
  (`jobs/{jobId}/{appId}.json`), capped at `MAX_REVIEWS` (default 10,000).
- **State:** Per-job status lives on the shared `Job` row (DynamoDB via AppSync),
  written by both Lambdas through conditional mutations. The frontend watches it
  in real time via `observeQuery`.
- **Downstream:** On success the Scraper emits a custom EventBridge event
  (`reviewlensai.scraper` / `ScrapeSucceeded`) on the `reviewlensai` bus, which
  Phase 2 analytics will consume (see `analytics/docs/CONTEXT.md`).

See the canonical interface (Validator request/response, `Job` fields,
`ScrapeSucceeded` schema) in `scraper/docs/API_CONTRACT.md`.

## Tech Stack (as built)

- AWS Lambda — Python 3.12 (Validator + async Scraper)
- AWS S3 (storage, 30-day lifecycle on `jobs/*`)
- AWS EventBridge (custom `reviewlensai` bus, `ScrapeSucceeded` event)
- AWS SQS (Scraper async-invoke DLQ) + CloudWatch alarms
- AWS AppSync (canonical `Job` write API, `x-api-key` auth)
- AWS CDK (TypeScript, IaC) — deployed via GitHub Actions
- Steam JSON endpoints: `appdetails` (metadata) + `appreviews` (reviews)

aiTitle: Set up Steam reviews analytics app repository

input.content:
# Scraper - Architecture

> As-built for Phase 1. The original Batch/Fargate/ECR + JobWatchdog design was
> replaced with two Lambdas during brainstorming (simpler, cheaper, no Docker/ECR
> for a ≤10k-review cap). See `docs/specs/2026-06-01-phase-1-design.md`.

## AWS resources (CDK: `scraper/cdk/lib/scraper-stack.ts`)

- **Validator Lambda** (Python 3.12, 256 MB, 10s) with a public Function URL
  (`AuthType: NONE`). Validates URL shape (host `store.steampowered.com`,
  `/app/{digits}`) then confirms the game via a Steam `appdetails` call. On
  success: creates `Job(PENDING)` (seeded with name/header/price), **async-invokes
  the Scraper** (`InvocationType: Event`, passing the fetched `appdetails`
  payload), and returns `{jobId}`. **CORS is owned entirely by the Function URL
  CORS config** (`allowedOrigins` = the Amplify URL from SSM `/reviewlensai/amplify/url`,
  `allowedMethods: [POST]`) — the handler sets no CORS headers (duplicate
  `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` headers break the browser; `OPTIONS` is not a
  valid Function-URL method and the preflight is auto-handled).
- **Scraper Lambda** (Python 3.12, 1024 MB, 600s, **reserved concurrency 3**,
  async **retryAttempts 0**, on-failure → SQS DLQ). Guards `PENDING→RUNNING`,
  paginates `appreviews` (dedup by `recommendationid`, stop on empty/repeated
  cursor or `MAX_REVIEWS=10000`), writes `jobs/{jobId}/{appId}.json` to S3, flips
  `Job→SUCCEEDED`, and emits `ScrapeSucceeded` (non-fatal) to the custom bus.
- **S3 bucket** for raw scrape output, `jobs/{jobId}/{appId}.json`,
  `BlockPublicAccess: ALL`, 30-day lifecycle on `jobs/*`.
- **Custom EventBridge bus** `reviewlensai` (name exported to SSM
  `/reviewlensai/scraper/eventBusName` for Phase 2). The Validator's Function URL
  is exported to SSM `/reviewlensai/scraper/validatorUrl`.
- **SQS DLQ** + two **CloudWatch alarms** (DLQ depth, Scraper `Errors`;
  `treatMissingData: NOT_BREACHING`).
- **CloudWatch logs**, 14-day retention, on both Lambdas.

## Runtime behaviors

- **No AWS Batch, no ECR, no JobWatchdog, no HEAD pre-check.** Game existence is
  the `appdetails` `success` check in the Validator. Soft scrape failures are
  handled by the Scraper's `try/except → Job FAILED`; hard crashes go to the DLQ
  (alarmed); the FE provides a client-side staleness timeout (no server sweep).
- **AppSync is the canonical write API for every `Job` row.** Validator and
  Scraper write via mutations with **API-key auth** (`x-api-key`, key from SSM
  `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey`) — no IAM/SigV4. All status transitions are
  conditional (guarded on the prior status); a guard miss is a no-op.
- **Failure taxonomy** (closed `errorMessage` set): "Couldn't reach Steam. Try
  again." / "Couldn't read Steam reviews. Try again." / "Couldn't start the
  scrape. Try again." / "Scrape failed. Try again." Zero-review games are a valid
  `SUCCEEDED` (`pctPositive` null).
- **Throttle behavior:** at reserved-concurrency=3 the async invoke still returns
  202; the throttled event routes to the DLQ and the row stays `PENDING` until the
  FE staleness timeout (not a Validator-observed error).
- **Networking.** Plain Lambda (AWS-managed internet egress; no VPC/NAT).
- **Observability.** Structured JSON logs with `jobId` per line.

## Deployment

`.github/workflows/scraper-deploy.yml` (GitHub Actions, OIDC role): pytest + ruff
→ build a clean Lambda asset (`build/`, vendoring `requests`) → CDK unit tests +
`cdk deploy` (reads app SSM params at synth) → triggers the app frontend rebuild
so `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL` is baked in. App must be deployed first (spec §6 order).

# Scraper - Architecture

## AWS resources (CDK: `scraper/cdk/lib/scraper-stack.ts`)

- **Validator Lambda** with public Function URL (`AuthType: NONE`, CORS allow-list from SSM `/reviewlensai/amplify/url`). Validates URL shape + does a HEAD pre-check against steam.com. 10s timeout.
- **AWS Batch (Fargate)** compute environment (1 vCPU, 2 GB) + job queue + **one job definition**: `WorkerJobDefinition` (GAME, 5min).
- **ECR repository** `reviewlensai-worker` — single image, used by the job definition.
- **S3 bucket** for raw scrape output, `jobs/{jobId}/{app_id}.json`. 30-day lifecycle on `jobs/*`.
- **JobWatchdog Lambda** + EventBridge rule filtered to `aws.batch` `Batch Job State Change` events with `status: FAILED` on our queue. Flips orphaned `RUNNING` `Job` rows to `FAILED` via a conditional AppSync mutation (`condition: { status: { eq: 'RUNNING' } }`).
- **CloudWatch logs**, 14-day retention, on Validator + Batch task + Watchdog.

## Runtime behaviors

- **Validator HEAD pre-check** is best-effort. Only `HTTP 404/410` and DNS/connect failures fail fast as `Game does not exist.`. Anti-bot blocks (403/503), timeouts, and unrecognized errors pass through. Env var `ENABLE_HEAD_PRECHECK=false` disables it without redeploy.
- **AppSync is the canonical write API for every `Job` row.** Validator, Worker, and Watchdog all write via mutations with API key auth. No direct DynamoDB writes.
- **Networking.** Default VPC, public subnets, `assignPublicIp: true` on Fargate. No NAT.
- **Observability.** Structured JSON logs with `jobId` per line.

# App - Architecture

## Domain Resources

The App Domain contains the following main resources:

- A React + Vite SPA deployed via AWS Amplify Hosting
- An AppSync GraphQL API auto-generated from the Amplify Data Schema
- A DynamoDB `Job` table (Amplify-managed, PAY_PER_REQUEST, TTL on `expiresAt`) that stores per-job state shared between the app and the scraper domain
- The Amplify Gen 2 backend definition that provisions all of the above

The app is a pure consumer of every other domain: it submits to the scraper's
Validator Lambda Function URL and otherwise reads state from the `Job` row in
DynamoDB via AppSync.

## Real-time updates

The frontend subscribes to a single `Job` row by id via Amplify Data's
`client.models.Job.observeQuery({ id })`. AppSync handles the subscription
plumbing; DynamoDB Streams are not used.

**Backend writers call AppSync mutations directly.** The Validator Lambda and
the Batch worker both call AppSync over HTTPS, authenticating with the
Amplify-issued AppSync API key (`x-api-key` header), and invoke `createJob` /
`updateJob` mutations. The frontend's subscription fires whenever any writer
mutates the row.

## Authorization model

The `Job` schema declares a single authorization mode:

- `allow.publicApiKey()` — used by the browser SPA, the Validator Lambda, and
  the Batch worker. The API key is generated by Amplify, shipped in the
  Amplify-generated `amplify_outputs.json` for the SPA, and written to SSM
  Parameter Store (`/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey`) for the scraper domain to
  consume.

This is a deliberate PoC trade-off: the API key is exposed in the FE bundle,
which means anyone with the key can read or mutate the `Job` table. The PoC
holds no PII and no sensitive data; this is acceptable. A real app would
introduce Cognito + per-mutation Schema-level `allow.resource(...)` or
`allow.authenticated('userPools')` to lock down writes to trusted
principals.

The original spec called for `allow.authenticated('iam')` on backend writers
plus SigV4 signing. That was simplified during  execution because the
Amplify Gen 2 IAM auth pathway requires either a Cognito Identity Pool (which
this project does not define) or a CDK escape hatch to add `AWS_IAM` as an
additional auth provider. Neither was necessary for the PoC.

## Cross-domain contract

The app's Amplify backend writes four SSM parameters at deploy time, which
the scraper domain reads at synth time:

- `/reviewlensai/appsync/url` — AppSync GraphQL endpoint
- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiId` — AppSync API ID
- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey` — AppSync API key (for backend writers)
- `/reviewlensai/amplify/url` — the deployed hosting URL (used by the
  scraper's Validator Lambda Function URL for the CORS allow-list, and by
  Playwright E2E as the base URL)

The app receives the Validator's URL via the Amplify branch environment
variable `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`, set by the scraper deploy workflow after the
scraper stack creates the Lambda Function URL.

## Local development

The SPA runs against MSW (handlers for the Validator URL) and a
`FakeAmplifyClient` (in-memory `Job` model that simulates the
`PENDING → RUNNING → SUCCEEDED` lifecycle on a timer). No AWS resources are
touched in local development. Staging is the only integration surface.

## Observability

CloudWatch log group for the Amplify build/deploy with 14-day retention.

# App - Architecture

## Domain Resources

The App Domain contains the following main resources:

- A React + Vite SPA deployed via AWS Amplify Hosting
- An AppSync GraphQL API auto-generated from the Amplify Data Schema
- A DynamoDB `Job` table (Amplify-managed, PAY_PER_REQUEST, TTL on `expiresAt`) that stores per-job state shared between the app and the scraper domain
- The Amplify Gen 2 backend definition that provisions all of the above

The app is a pure consumer of every other domain: it submits to the scraper's
Validator Lambda Function URL and otherwise reads state from the `Job` row in
DynamoDB via AppSync.

## Real-time updates

The frontend subscribes to a single `Job` row by id via Amplify Data's
`client.models.Job.observeQuery({ id })`. AppSync handles the subscription
plumbing; DynamoDB Streams are not used.

**Backend writers call AppSync mutations directly.** The Validator Lambda and
the async Scraper Lambda both call AppSync over HTTPS, authenticating with the
Amplify-issued AppSync API key (`x-api-key` header), and invoke `createJob` /
`updateJob` mutations (the latter with the full `Job` selectionSet and a
conditional guard on the prior status). The frontend's subscription fires
whenever any writer mutates the row.

## Authorization model

The `Job` schema declares a single authorization mode:

- `allow.publicApiKey()` — used by the browser SPA, the Validator Lambda, and
  the Batch worker. The API key is generated by Amplify, shipped in the
  Amplify-generated `amplify_outputs.json` for the SPA, and written to SSM
  Parameter Store (`/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey`) for the scraper domain to
  consume.

This is a deliberate PoC trade-off: the API key is exposed in the FE bundle,
which means anyone with the key can read or mutate the `Job` table. The PoC
holds no PII and no sensitive data; this is acceptable. A real app would
introduce Cognito + per-mutation Schema-level `allow.resource(...)` or
`allow.authenticated('userPools')` to lock down writes to trusted
principals.

The original spec called for `allow.authenticated('iam')` on backend writers
plus SigV4 signing. That was simplified during  execution because the
Amplify Gen 2 IAM auth pathway requires either a Cognito Identity Pool (which
this project does not define) or a CDK escape hatch to add `AWS_IAM` as an
additional auth provider. Neither was necessary for the PoC.

## Cross-domain contract

The app's Amplify backend writes four SSM parameters at deploy time, which
the scraper domain reads at synth time:

- `/reviewlensai/appsync/url` — AppSync GraphQL endpoint
- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiId` — AppSync API ID
- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey` — AppSync API key (for backend writers)
- `/reviewlensai/amplify/url` — the deployed hosting URL (used by the
  scraper's Validator Lambda Function URL for the CORS allow-list, and by
  Playwright E2E as the base URL)

The app receives the Validator's URL via the Amplify branch environment
variable `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`, set by the scraper deploy workflow after the
scraper stack creates the Lambda Function URL.

## Local development

The SPA runs against MSW (handlers for the Validator URL) and a
`FakeAmplifyClient` (in-memory `Job` model that simulates the
`PENDING → RUNNING → SUCCEEDED` lifecycle on a timer). No AWS resources are
touched in local development. Staging is the only integration surface.

## Observability

CloudWatch log group for the Amplify build/deploy with 14-day retention.

input.new_string:
The app's Amplify backend writes these SSM parameters at deploy time, which
the scraper domain reads at synth time:

- `/reviewlensai/appsync/url` — AppSync GraphQL endpoint
- `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey` — AppSync API key (for backend writers)

  API, used by the Validator Function URL CORS allow-list and by Playwright E2E
  as the base URL)

(The original `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiId` param was dropped — it only existed
to build IAM field-ARNs, which the API-key auth model doesn't need.)

The Validator's Function URL flows back the other way: the scraper stack exports
it to SSM `/reviewlensai/scraper/validatorUrl`, and the app deploy workflow reads
that param and bakes it into the frontend build as `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`
(rebuilding after the scraper deploys).

## Deployment

The app deploys via `.github/workflows/app-deploy.yml` (GitHub Actions, OIDC
role): lint/typecheck/test → `ampx pipeline-deploy` (backend, regenerates
`amplify_outputs.json`) → publish the hosting URL to SSM → `npm run build`
(static-imports the real outputs + `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL`) → deploy `dist/` to
Amplify Hosting via the manual zip-deployment API. The scraper workflow
re-triggers this to bake the Validator URL.

# Review Lens AI Design and Architecture

## System Architecture

### Overview

Review Lens AI is a monorepo project deployed via GitHub actions onto AWS infrastructure. The frontend of the app is a React app deployed onto AWS Amplify. Each of the various functionalities (scraper, analytics, chat) will be separate backend domains that the app will interact with.

### Scraper

The scraper service's main technical domain are AWS Batch jobs that can be triggered to scrape Steam games review data and put the data onto AWS S3. The frontend will trigger the scraper, track it, and query the S3 data via a set of Lambda functions. See `scraper/docs/CONTEXT.md` for more details.

### Analytics

Triggered by the scraper's Batch SUCCEEDED EventBridge event, an AnalyticsSubmitter Lambda queues a Python Analytics Batch job on Fargate. The Batch job runs over the S3 data that was scraped and produces summarized data analytics (sentiment series, word associations, helpful reviews) which it writes back onto the same `Job` row via AppSync mutation for the frontend to subscribe to and display. See `analytics/docs/CONTEXT.md` for more details.

### Chat

The AI Chat bot is hosted via AWS Bedrock Agent (Amazon Nova Pro,
us-east-1) with a Bedrock Knowledge Base backed by Aurora Serverless v2 +
pgvector. Two Node 20 Lambdas: `ChatIngester` (EventBridge-triggered on
analytics SUCCEEDED, uploads docs + sidecar metadata, kicks off
`StartIngestionJob`) and `ChatTurn` (FE-facing Function URL, sanitizes input,
invokes the agent via the `invokeAgentForJob` wrapper with a server-derived
`jobId` metadata filter, streams chunks back via AppSync `ChatMessage`
mutations with a full `selectionSet` so subscriptions deliver complete
rows). Bedrock Guardrails attached at the agent level (denied topics,
content filters at HIGH, PII anonymize, contextual grounding). Lambda
output post-filters layer on top to handle Nova-specific edge cases
(non-English mirror, system-prompt sentinel detection, Steam-platform
synonym scope drift, PII token stripping). See `chat/docs/CONTEXT.md`
for the per-component build details.

### Deployment

Each domain of the application will use it's own CI/CD via Github actions and AWS CDK. This will create separate CloudFormation stacks and resources. Each domain will interact with each other via a variety of Lambdas and Event Queues that are defined in each repo's `docs/API_CONTRACT.md`.

### Auth

This a PoC app, there will be no auth services.

### Testing

Besides unit and integration testing. Scraper and analytics functionality
can be tested E2E via AWS CLI. Chat is red-teamed locally via a Playwright
+ API-direct adversarial-PM subagent (`.claude/agents/adversarial-pm.md`)
against a corpus that'll be set and grown through exploratory testing.

## Project Phases

Some of the work of this project can be done in parallel, while some of the work require sequential effort. For each phase of work, refer to the relevant docs in each domain's `docs/product/prds/`

### Phase 1: Scraper and basic App functionality

In this first phase, these two domains can be setup in parallel. Here, the full scraper functionality can be setup, while a functional React App can be setup to integrate with the Scraper.

### Phase 2: Analytics and Chat

In this second phase, these two domains can be setup in parallel. The analytics functionality can be developed alongside the chat, with the chat functionality initial knowledge base being from the scraped data. Once the analytics functionality is finished, then the chat bot can integrate the analytics into it's own knowledge base.

### Phase 3: Chat domain end-to-end

Stood up the full chat stack: Bedrock Agent (Amazon Nova Pro via US
cross-region inference profile), Bedrock Knowledge Base (Aurora Serverless
v2 + pgvector backing, Titan v2 1024-dim embeddings, NONE chunking),
Bedrock Guardrails (denied topics, content filters, PII anonymize,
contextual grounding), `ChatIngester` + `ChatTurn` Lambdas, FE ChatDrawer
+ ChatPanel + Citations + chat subscription hook. Schema additions
(`Job.chatStatus`, `Job.chatErrorMessage`, `ChatMessage` model) merged to
main.

### Phase 4: Tuning and additional functionality (future)

Once Phase 3 has hardened in real user testing, additional tuning per
domain can be developed in parallel.

# Review Lens AI Design and Architecture

## System Architecture

### Overview

Review Lens AI is a monorepo project deployed via GitHub actions onto AWS infrastructure. The frontend of the app is a React app deployed onto AWS Amplify. Each of the various functionalities (scraper, analytics, chat) will be separate backend domains that the app will interact with.

### Scraper

The scraper service runs as two Python Lambdas — a synchronous **Validator**
(public Function URL) and an asynchronous **Scraper** — that scrape Steam game
review data and put it onto AWS S3 (capped at 10k reviews/game). The frontend
triggers the Validator, then tracks the job in real time via the shared `Job`
row (DynamoDB/AppSync). (The original Batch/Fargate/ECR design was replaced with
Lambda in Phase 1 for simplicity.) See `scraper/docs/CONTEXT.md` for more details.

### Analytics

Triggered by the scraper's Batch SUCCEEDED EventBridge event, an AnalyticsSubmitter Lambda queues a Python Analytics Batch job on Fargate. The Batch job runs over the S3 data that was scraped and produces summarized data analytics (sentiment series, word associations, helpful reviews) which it writes back onto the same `Job` row via AppSync mutation for the frontend to subscribe to and display. See `analytics/docs/CONTEXT.md` for more details.

### Chat

The AI Chat bot is hosted via AWS Bedrock Agent (Amazon Nova Pro,
us-east-1) with a Bedrock Knowledge Base backed by Aurora Serverless v2 +
pgvector. Two Node 20 Lambdas: `ChatIngester` (EventBridge-triggered on
analytics SUCCEEDED, uploads docs + sidecar metadata, kicks off
`StartIngestionJob`) and `ChatTurn` (FE-facing Function URL, sanitizes input,
invokes the agent via the `invokeAgentForJob` wrapper with a server-derived
`jobId` metadata filter, streams chunks back via AppSync `ChatMessage`
mutations with a full `selectionSet` so subscriptions deliver complete
rows). Bedrock Guardrails attached at the agent level (denied topics,
content filters at HIGH, PII anonymize, contextual grounding). Lambda
output post-filters layer on top to handle Nova-specific edge cases
(non-English mirror, system-prompt sentinel detection, Steam-platform
synonym scope drift, PII token stripping). See `chat/docs/CONTEXT.md`
for the per-component build details.

### Deployment

Each domain of the application will use it's own CI/CD via Github actions and AWS CDK. This will create separate CloudFormation stacks and resources. Each domain will interact with each other via a variety of Lambdas and Event Queues that are defined in each repo's `docs/API_CONTRACT.md`.

### Auth

This a PoC app, there will be no auth services.

### Testing

Besides unit and integration testing. Scraper and analytics functionality
can be tested E2E via AWS CLI. Chat is red-teamed locally via a Playwright
+ API-direct adversarial-PM subagent (`.claude/agents/adversarial-pm.md`)
against a corpus that'll be set and grown through exploratory testing.

## Project Phases

Some of the work of this project can be done in parallel, while some of the work require sequential effort. For each phase of work, refer to the relevant docs in each domain's `docs/product/prds/`

### Phase 1: Scraper and basic App functionality

In this first phase, these two domains can be setup in parallel. Here, the full scraper functionality can be setup, while a functional React App can be setup to integrate with the Scraper.

### Phase 2: Analytics and Chat

In this second phase, these two domains can be setup in parallel. The analytics functionality can be developed alongside the chat, with the chat functionality initial knowledge base being from the scraped data. Once the analytics functionality is finished, then the chat bot can integrate the analytics into it's own knowledge base.

### Phase 3: Chat domain end-to-end

Stood up the full chat stack: Bedrock Agent (Amazon Nova Pro via US
cross-region inference profile), Bedrock Knowledge Base (Aurora Serverless
v2 + pgvector backing, Titan v2 1024-dim embeddings, NONE chunking),
Bedrock Guardrails (denied topics, content filters, PII anonymize,
contextual grounding), `ChatIngester` + `ChatTurn` Lambdas, FE ChatDrawer
+ ChatPanel + Citations + chat subscription hook. Schema additions
(`Job.chatStatus`, `Job.chatErrorMessage`, `ChatMessage` model) merged to
main.

### Phase 4: Tuning and additional functionality (future)

Once Phase 3 has hardened in real user testing, additional tuning per
domain can be developed in parallel.

# Analytics — Context

## Purpose

Turn raw scraped reviews into surface-level analytics the frontend can display,
using classical ML techniques (no LLMs in this domain).

## Domain Boundary

- **Trigger:** Job-complete event emitted by `scraper/`.
- **Compute:** AWS Batch job reads raw reviews from S3.
- **Output:** Summarized analytics written to a DynamoDB table.
- **Downstream:**
  - `app/` reads summarized analytics via Lambdas in front of DynamoDB.
  - `chat/` consumes analytics output as a Bedrock knowledge base.

## Tech Stack

- AWS Batch on Fargate (job runner) — Python 3.12
- AWS Lambda (Node 20, AnalyticsSubmitter) — EventBridge-triggered
- AWS EventBridge (scraper-SUCCEEDED → Submitter)
- AWS S3 (input, owned by `scraper/`)
- AWS AppSync (output sink — writes back onto the same `Job` row)
- AWS CDK (TypeScript, IaC)
- Classical ML: NLTK VADER (sentiment), spaCy `en_core_web_sm` (word associations)

lines: +- **Trigger:** the scraper's custom `ScrapeSucceeded` EventBridge event on the
+  > As-built note (Phase 1 changed the scraper): the scraper is now Lambda-based,
+  > so there is **no Batch `Job State Change` event and no `jobQueueArn`** to key
+  > off. This file's older Batch-trigger references below predate that change and
+  > will be reconciled when Phase 2 is specced.
- **Compute:** AWS Batch job reads raw reviews from S3.
- **Output:** Summarized analytics written to a DynamoDB table.

input.new_string:

> Batch). The cross-domain trigger below — an `aws.batch` `Batch Job State Change`
> rule keyed on `/reviewlensai/scraper/jobQueueArn` — is **superseded**: the
> scraper now emits a custom `ScrapeSucceeded` event on the `reviewlensai` bus
> (`/reviewlensai/scraper/eventBusName`). The analytics *worker* may still run on
> Batch/Fargate; only the scraper→analytics trigger changed. This doc is Phase 2
> aspirational and will be reconciled when Phase 2 is specced.

## AWS resources (CDK: `analytics/cdk/lib/analytics-stack.ts`)

> Batch/Fargate; only the scraper→analytics trigger changed. This doc is Phase 2
> aspirational and will be reconciled when Phase 2 is specced.

## AWS resources (CDK: `analytics/cdk/lib/analytics-stack.ts`)
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Analytics - Architecture

## AWS resources (CDK: `analytics/cdk/lib/analytics-stack.ts`)

- **AnalyticsSubmitter Lambda** (Node 20, arm64, 256 MB, 15s timeout). No public URL — invoked exclusively by EventBridge. Reads the `Job` row via AppSync `getJob`, gates idempotency, writes `analyticsStatus: PENDING`, then calls `Batch.SubmitJob`.
- **EventBridge rule** `AnalyticsSubmitterRule` filtered to `aws.batch` `Batch Job State Change` events where `detail.status: SUCCEEDED` AND `detail.jobQueue` matches the scraper's queue ARN (read at synth from SSM `/reviewlensai/scraper/jobQueueArn`). Target: the Submitter Lambda.
- **AWS Batch (Fargate)** compute environment (1 vCPU, 4 GB, `maxvCpus: 4` headroom) + job queue + single `AnalyticsJobDefinition`. 30-minute timeout, 1 retry attempt. Container ENTRYPOINT runs `python -m analytics_worker.main`; Batch passes `--job-id`, `--s3-keys` via Ref parameters (`Ref::jobId`, `Ref::s3Keys`).
- **ECR repository** `reviewlensai-analytics` — imported by the stack (pre-created by the deploy workflow), holds the worker image at tag `latest`.
- **CloudWatch log groups**, 14-day retention, on Submitter Lambda and Batch task (stream prefix `analytics-worker`).
- **IAM roles:**
  - `AnalyticsTaskExecutionRole` — `AmazonECSTaskExecutionRolePolicy` + `ecr:Pull` on the analytics ECR repo.
  - `AnalyticsJobRole` (task role) — explicit `s3:GetObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::<scrapeBucket>/jobs/*` and `appsync:GraphQL` on the `updateJob` field ARN.
  - Submitter execution role — `appsync:GraphQL` on `getJob` and `updateJob` field ARNs + `batch:SubmitJob` on the analytics queue / job-definition ARNs.

## Cross-domain SSM dependencies consumed

All read at synth time via `StringParameter.valueForStringParameter`:

| Parameter                              | Producer  | Used for                                                              |
|----------------------------------------|-----------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `/reviewlensai/appsync/url`            | `app/`    | AppSync endpoint baked into Submitter Lambda and Batch task env       |
| `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiId`          | `app/`    | Constructs the field-level ARN `/types/.../fields/{getJob,updateJob}` |
| `/reviewlensai/appsync/apiKey`         | `app/`    | API-key auth for `getJob` / `updateJob` calls                         |
| `/reviewlensai/scraper/jobQueueArn`    | `scraper/`| EventBridge `detail.jobQueue` filter (only fire on scraper-queue events) |
| `/reviewlensai/scraper/bucketName`     | `scraper/`| IAM `s3:GetObject` grant + worker runtime `S3_BUCKET` env var         |

Both scraper-side SSM params are produced by `scraper/cdk/lib/scraper-stack.ts` (`ScraperJobQueueArnSsmParam`, `ScraperBucketNameSsmParam`). They must exist before this stack is deployed.

## Runtime behaviors

- **Submitter idempotency.** Submitter exits as a no-op if any of the following hold (logged with a `submitter_skipped_*` event so each skip is observable):
  - `event.detail.parameters.jobId` is missing — emits `submitter_skipped_no_jobid`.
  - `getJob` returns null — emits `submitter_skipped_no_row`.
  - Row `status !== 'SUCCEEDED'` — emits `submitter_skipped_status` (defends against future detail-status changes).
  - Row `analyticsStatus !== null` — emits `submitter_skipped_idempotent`. The EventBridge → Lambda async invoke can deliver duplicates; this gate is the deduplication.
  - `s3Keys` empty or null — emits `submitter_skipped_no_s3keys`. Nothing to analyze.
- **Submitter ordering (Patch #2).** Submitter writes `analyticsStatus: PENDING` via `updateJob` BEFORE calling `Batch.SubmitJob`. Inverting the order can race: the worker can start and write `analyticsStatus: RUNNING` before the PENDING write lands, making the FE subscription regress (`RUNNING` → `PENDING`). The trade-off: a Batch-submit failure leaves a row stuck at `PENDING`; the future `AnalyticsWatchdog` (spec §10, deferred) will rescue these.
- **Worker dispatch.** `main.py` runs `run_game_analytics(s3_keys, reader)` — sentiment series (weekly/monthly/all-time), word associations (adjectives + phrases), top-3 helpful positive/negative reviews. GAME jobs always have exactly one S3 object.
- **Error taxonomy.** Worker writes one of three constrained `analyticsErrorMessage` strings on FAILED — never raw exception text:
  - `"No reviews to analyze."` — `NoReviewsError` (zero S3 docs or game has zero reviews).
  - `"Could not read scrape data."` — `S3ReadError` (S3 GetObject failure, JSON parse failure).
  - `"Analytics worker failed."` — catch-all for any other exception.
- **Retry policy.**
  - AppSync `updateJob` (worker): 1 retry on 5xx / network error with 500ms backoff. 4xx is NOT retried (caller-side input error).
  - AppSync calls (Submitter): same 1-retry-with-500ms policy.
  - S3 `GetObject` (worker): boto3 default retry config (standard mode, 3 attempts).
  - `Batch.SubmitJob` (Submitter): AWS SDK default retry config.
- **§11 fallback (analytics payload as JSON string).** `@aws-amplify/backend@1.4.0` does not support `a.customType()` arrays, so the analytics payload is stored as a single JSON-stringified string on `Job.analyticsJson` instead of a nested custom type. The Python worker calls `json.dumps(payload)` with camelCase keys; the FE parses via `parseAnalytics()` in `app/src/types/analytics.ts`, which returns `AnalyticsPayload | null` (tolerant — returns null on parse error or non-object payload). The hand-rolled TypeScript interface in `app/src/types/analytics.ts` is the single source of truth for the payload shape; the FE renderer, the FakeAmplifyClient fixtures, and the Python worker all conform to it.

## Observability

Structured JSON logs with `jobId` per line (helper: `analytics_worker.log.log_json` / `submitter/index.ts logJson`). Event vocabulary (spec §8):

- **Submitter**
  - `submitter_invoked` (jobId, eventDetail)
  - `submitter_skipped_no_jobid`
  - `submitter_skipped_no_row` (jobId)
  - `submitter_skipped_status` (jobId, status)
  - `submitter_skipped_idempotent` (jobId, analyticsStatus)
  - `submitter_skipped_no_s3keys` (jobId)
  - `submitter_submitted` (jobId, batchJobId)
  - `submitter_submit_failed` (jobId, error)
- **Worker**
  - `worker_start` (jobId, s3Keys count)
  - `sentiment_complete` (weeklyPoints, monthlyPoints, allTimePoints)
  - `words_complete` (adjectives, phrases)
  - `helpful_complete` (positive, negative)
  - `worker_complete` (jobId)
  - `worker_no_reviews` (jobId) — terminal FAILED with `"No reviews to analyze."`
  - `worker_s3_read_failed` (jobId, error) — terminal FAILED with `"Could not read scrape data."`
  - `worker_failed` (jobId, error) — terminal FAILED with `"Analytics worker failed."`
  - `worker_failed_terminal_write_failed` (jobId, error) — the FAILED write itself failed; exit 1
- **AppSync client (worker + submitter)**
  - `appsync_call_start` (attempt, jobId, analyticsStatus)
  - `appsync_call_complete` (jobId)
  - `appsync_call_retrying` (jobId)
  - `appsync_call_failed` (attempt, jobId, error)

+> (`/reviewlensai/scraper/eventBusName`). The analytics *worker* may still run on
+> Batch/Fargate; only the scraper→analytics trigger changed. This doc is Phase 2
+> aspirational and will be reconciled when Phase 2 is specced.

- **AnalyticsSubmitter Lambda** (Node 20, arm64, 256 MB, 15s timeout). No public URL — invoked exclusively by EventBridge. Reads the `Job` row via AppSync `getJob`, gates idempotency, writes `analyticsStatus: PENDING`, then calls `Batch.SubmitJob`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
name: app-deploy
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["app/**", ".github/workflows/app-deploy.yml"]
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: app
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"
          cache: "npm"
          cache-dependency-path: app/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm test
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - name: Deploy Amplify Gen 2 backend (regenerates amplify_outputs.json)
        run: npx ampx pipeline-deploy --branch main --app-id ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }}
      - name: Publish Amplify hosting URL to SSM (read from the API, never string-templated)
        run: |
          DOMAIN=$(aws amplify get-app --app-id ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }} --query 'app.defaultDomain' --output text)
          URL="https://main.${DOMAIN}"
          # Fail loudly if the URL is malformed — it becomes the Validator CORS origin (spec §6).
          case "$URL" in https://main.*.amplifyapp.com) ;; *) echo "Bad Amplify URL: $URL" && exit 1 ;; esac
          aws ssm put-parameter --name /reviewlensai/amplify/url --type String --overwrite --value "$URL"
      - name: Build frontend (bundles real outputs + validator URL if the scraper is deployed)
        run: |
          VURL=$(aws ssm get-parameter --name /reviewlensai/scraper/validatorUrl --query Parameter.Value --output text 2>/dev/null || echo "")
          echo "VITE_VALIDATOR_URL=${VURL:-<not set yet>}"
          VITE_VALIDATOR_URL="$VURL" npm run build
      - name: Deploy frontend to Amplify Hosting (manual zip deployment)
        env:
          APP_ID: ${{ secrets.AMPLIFY_APP_ID }}
        run: |
          (cd dist && zip -qr ../dist.zip .)
          OUT=$(aws amplify create-deployment --app-id "$APP_ID" --branch-name main)
          JOB_ID=$(echo "$OUT" | jq -r '.jobId')
          UPLOAD_URL=$(echo "$OUT" | jq -r '.zipUploadUrl')
          curl -s -H "Content-Type: application/zip" --upload-file dist.zip "$UPLOAD_URL"
          aws amplify start-deployment --app-id "$APP_ID" --branch-name main --job-id "$JOB_ID"
          echo "Frontend deployment started (job $JOB_ID)."

toolUseResult.originalFile:
name: scraper-deploy
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["scraper/**", ".github/workflows/scraper-deploy.yml"]
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
  actions: write
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Unit tests + lint
        working-directory: scraper
        run: |
          python -m pip install -e ".[dev]"
          python -m pytest -q
          ruff check src tests scripts

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: "20"

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: "${{ secrets.AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}"
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Build Lambda asset (CLEAN build dir — never the source tree)
        working-directory: scraper
        run: |
          rm -rf build && mkdir -p build
          cp -r src/reviewlensai_scraper build/
          pip install requests -t build/
        # boto3 is in the Lambda runtime; only `requests` is vendored into the clean build/
        # dir. This matches Code.fromAsset("../build") in the CDK stack.

      - name: CDK unit tests + deploy (reads app SSM params at synth — app must be deployed first)
        working-directory: scraper/cdk
        run: |
          npm ci
          npm test           # jest stack assertions (../build exists from the prior step)
          npx cdk deploy --require-approval never
        # The CDK stack writes /reviewlensai/scraper/validatorUrl to SSM.

      - name: Trigger app frontend rebuild (picks up the new validatorUrl)
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run app-deploy.yml --ref main \
            || echo "Could not auto-trigger app-deploy; re-run it manually to bake VITE_VALIDATOR_URL."

content.text:
Done. To directly answer your question: **no, I hadn't — and I've now fixed that.** Here's what I updated to match the as-built (Lambda) reality:

## Docs synced to as-built
| Doc | Change |
|---|---|
| `scraper/docs/CONTEXT.md` | Rewritten: two Lambdas (not Batch), S3 cap, custom `ScrapeSucceeded` event, as-built tech stack |
| `scraper/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Rewritten: Validator + async Scraper Lambdas, **CORS owned by Function URL** (the bug we hit), DLQ/reserved-concurrency/alarms, custom bus, API-key auth, GitHub Actions deploy. Removed Batch/ECR/JobWatchdog/HEAD-precheck. |
| `app/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` | "Batch worker" → Scraper Lambda; dropped the removed `appsync/apiId` SSM param; corrected the `VITE_VALIDATOR_URL` flow; added the as-built GitHub Actions deploy section |
| `docs/OVERVIEW.md` | Scraper section → Lambda-based; analytics trigger → custom `ScrapeSucceeded` event |
| `analytics/docs/CONTEXT.md` + `ARCHITECTURE.md` | Added **as-built notes** flagging that the scraper→analytics trigger is now the custom `ScrapeSucceeded` event (not Batch `Job State Change`/`jobQueueArn`) — our Phase 1 change had silently invalidated their Phase 2 assumptions |

**Left intentionally unchanged:** `docs/prds/phase-1.md` (the original requirements snapshot — historical input, not an as-built doc), `app/docs/CONTEXT.md` (still accurate at its altitude), and `chat/docs/CONTEXT.md` (its "Batch" reference is to *analytics'* own Batch worker, a legitimate Phase 2 choice, not the scraper).

I also tightened both deploy workflows' path filters to **ignore `docs/**`**, so documentation changes no longer trigger redeploys (I cancelled the two runs this commit triggered — nothing functional changed, and they were cancelled in the early `npm ci` phase, well before any AWS step, so the live deployment is untouched).

The remaining workflow step is still CLAUDE.md §9 — **saving the scrubbed session transcript to `ai-transcripts/`** — which I flagged earlier and can do if you export the conversation or want a structured summary doc in its place.

input.content:
import re
import os

SRC = "C:/Users/nicho/.claude/projects/C--Users-nicho-OneDrive-Documents-Projects-reviewlensai/4957cd86-9a91-456d-8b86-e2281f40917b.jsonl"
DST_DIR = "C:/Users/nicho/OneDrive/Documents/Projects/reviewlensai/ai-transcripts"
DST = os.path.join(DST_DIR, "2026-06-01-phase-1-session.jsonl")

os.makedirs(DST_DIR, exist_ok=True)

# Scrub keys/secrets (CLAUDE.md). Endpoint URLs are already public via the FE bundle, but the
# AppSync API key, any GitHub/AWS tokens, and the account id are redacted to be safe in a public repo.
patterns = [
    (re.compile(r"da2-[A-Za-z0-9]{12,}"), "da2-REDACTED"),
    (re.compile(r"gh[oprsu]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}"), "REDACTED_GH_TOKEN"),
    (re.compile(r"AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}"), "REDACTED_AKIA"),
    (re.compile(r"ASIA[0-9A-Z]{16}"), "REDACTED_ASIA"),
    (re.compile(r"(?i)(aws_secret_access_key[\"'\s:=]+)[A-Za-z0-9/+]{30,}"), r"\1REDACTED"),
    (re.compile(r"\bREDACTED_ACCT\b"), "REDACTED_ACCT"),
]

lines = 0
total_redactions = 0
with open(SRC, encoding="utf-8") as f, open(DST, "w", encoding="utf-8") as o:
    for line in f:
        for pat, repl in patterns:
            line, c = pat.subn(repl, line)
            total_redactions += c
        o.write(line)
        lines += 1

print(f"wrote {DST}")
print(f"lines={lines} redactions={total_redactions} size={os.path.getsize(DST)} bytes")

    (re.compile(r"(?i)(aws_secret_access_key[\"'\s:=]+)[A-Za-z0-9/+]{30,}"), r"\1REDACTED"),
    (re.compile(r"\bREDACTED_ACCT\b"), "REDACTED_ACCT"),
]

lines = 0
total_redactions = 0
with open(SRC, encoding="utf-8") as f, open(DST, "w", encoding="utf-8") as o:
    for line in f:
        for pat, repl in patterns:
            line, c = pat.subn(repl, line)
            total_redactions += c
        o.write(line)
        lines += 1

print(f"wrote {DST}")
print(f"lines={lines} redactions={total_redactions} size={os.path.getsize(DST)} bytes")
